clinicalStem: A 45-year-old man presents with a 6-week history of epigastric pain that is worse before meals and partially relieved by eating. He takes ibuprofen occasionally for back pain. On examination, there is mild epigastric tenderness. Bloods show Hb 13.2 g/dL, WCC 7.1 x10⁹/L. He has not taken any antibiotics or proton pump inhibitors (PPIs) in the last 4 weeks. Which is the SINGLE BEST initial investigation to confirm the most likely diagnosis?
options: Stool antigen test for H. pylori
explanation: The urea breath test (UBT) is the preferred non-invasive first-line test for active H. pylori infection in a patient who has not recently used antibiotics or PPIs. It has sensitivity and specificity exceeding 95% and directly detects active infection rather than past exposure. Endoscopy is reserved for patients with alarm features or those aged >55 with new dyspepsia.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. The urea breath test detects active H. pylori infection with >95% sensitivity/specificity and is the first-line non-invasive test in this clinical scenario.
Endoscopy is the gold standard but is invasive and reserved for patients with alarm features (dysphagia, weight loss, haematemesis, anaemia) or age >55. This patient has none.
Serology (IgG) cannot distinguish active from past infection — it remains positive for years after eradication and is therefore not suitable for confirming current infection.

Stool antigen testing is an acceptable alternative to UBT for active infection, but it is second-line to UBT in most guidelines because laboratory turnaround is longer and patient compliance can vary.

Fasting serum gastrin is used to investigate Zollinger-Ellison syndrome (refractory peptic ulcer, secretory diarrhoea, multiple ulcers), which is not suggested by this straightforward clinical picture.
clinicalPearl: The urea breath test is the most accurate non-invasive test for ACTIVE H. pylori infection; serology reflects past exposure, not current status.
keyClue: Epigastric pain worse before meals, partially relieved by eating, no recent antibiotics or PPIs — classic peptic ulcer / H. pylori scenario with no alarm features.
commonExamTrap: Choosing serology (IgG) — it cannot confirm active infection and remains positive long after successful eradication.
missedConcept: Distinguishing tests that confirm ACTIVE infection (UBT, stool antigen) from those that only indicate past exposure (serology).

clinicalStem: A 62-year-old woman presents with a 3-month history of progressive difficulty swallowing solid food, a 7 kg unintentional weight loss, and early satiety. She is a non-smoker. On examination she appears cachectic with epigastric fullness. Bloods reveal Hb 9.4 g/dL (microcytic), albumin 27 g/L. She has not had any previous GI investigations. What is the SINGLE MOST APPROPRIATE next investigation?
explanation: This patient has multiple alarm features for upper GI malignancy: age >55, progressive dysphagia, significant weight loss, early satiety, and iron-deficiency anaemia. Upper GI endoscopy with biopsy is the investigation of choice — it directly visualises the mucosa, allows tissue diagnosis, and can stage local disease. CT is used for staging after a histological diagnosis is established.
wrongAnswerExplanations: CT is essential for staging once a diagnosis is confirmed histologically, but endoscopy with biopsy must come first to obtain tissue for diagnosis.
Correct. Urgent upper GI endoscopy with biopsy is the first-line investigation for this cluster of alarm features; it provides direct visualisation and histological diagnosis.
Barium swallow can demonstrate a filling defect but provides no histological diagnosis and would delay definitive investigation; it is not preferred over endoscopy when malignancy is suspected.
H. pylori testing is appropriate for uncomplicated dyspepsia but is wholly inadequate as the primary investigation when alarm features of malignancy are present.
PET scanning plays a role in staging or assessing treatment response in confirmed malignancy but has no role as the initial diagnostic investigation.
clinicalPearl: Any patient >55 with new dyspepsia PLUS an alarm feature (dysphagia, weight loss, anaemia, persistent vomiting, palpable mass) requires urgent endoscopy — never 'test-and-treat' for H. pylori first.
keyClue: Progressive dysphagia + 7 kg weight loss + microcytic anaemia + epigastric fullness in a 62-year-old = alarm features mandating urgent endoscopy.
commonExamTrap: Ordering CT first — CT stages disease but cannot provide histological diagnosis; endoscopy must precede staging.
missedConcept: The 2-week-wait alarm feature criteria that mandate urgent endoscopy rather than empirical PPI or H. pylori testing.

clinicalStem: A 38-year-old man presents with recurrent peptic ulcers despite adequate triple therapy for H. pylori eradication (confirmed negative on repeat UBT). He also reports persistent watery diarrhoea and a 5 kg weight loss over 3 months. Endoscopy shows multiple ulcers in the first and second parts of the duodenum and in the jejunum. Bloods: Hb 11.8 g/dL, Na 137, K 3.1 mmol/L. What is the SINGLE MOST LIKELY underlying diagnosis?
explanation: Zollinger-Ellison syndrome (ZES) is caused by a gastrin-secreting tumour (gastrinoma), most commonly in the pancreas or duodenum. It classically presents with recurrent, refractory peptic ulcers in atypical locations (distal duodenum, jejunum), secretory diarrhoea, and weight loss. The confirmed H. pylori eradication and failure of standard therapy, combined with jejunal ulcers, strongly point to ZES. Diagnosis is confirmed by elevated fasting serum gastrin.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Crohn's disease can cause small bowel ulceration but would not explain elevated acid secretion, and the secretory diarrhoea pattern with multiple duodeno-jejunal ulcers in an H. pylori-negative patient is more characteristic of ZES.
Correct. Recurrent duodenal and jejunal ulcers refractory to H. pylori eradication, combined with secretory diarrhoea, is the hallmark presentation of Zollinger-Ellison syndrome (gastrinoma).
Autoimmune gastritis (type A) destroys parietal cells, causing achlorhydria and pernicious anaemia — the opposite of the acid hypersecretion seen in ZES.
NSAID-induced ulcers are typically gastric or proximal duodenal; they would not explain jejunal ulcers or persistent secretory diarrhoea, and the patient is not described as using NSAIDs.

Hyperparathyroidism can be associated with MEN-1 (which includes gastrinomas), but hypercalcaemia alone does not cause recurrent jejunal ulcers; ZES from the gastrinoma component remains the primary explanation.
clinicalPearl: Multiple peptic ulcers in unusual locations (distal duodenum, jejunum) + secretory diarrhoea + failed H. pylori eradication = Zollinger-Ellison syndrome until proven otherwise; check fasting serum gastrin.
keyClue: Multiple ulcers extending to the jejunum with secretory diarrhoea despite confirmed H. pylori eradication.
commonExamTrap: Diagnosing Crohn's disease — Crohn's does not cause acid hypersecretion or gastrinoma-level ulcer burden.
missedConcept: The gastrinoma triangle and the characteristic jejunal ulceration pattern in ZES that distinguishes it from standard H. pylori peptic ulcer disease.

clinicalStem: A 28-year-old woman presents with a 1-year history of bloating, offensive loose stools, and fatigue. She has lost 6 kg over this period. Blood tests reveal: Hb 10.2 g/dL (MCV 72 fL), ferritin 6 µg/L, folate 2.1 nmol/L. She is currently on a gluten-containing diet. What is the SINGLE BEST initial serological test to establish the diagnosis?
explanation: IgA anti-tTG antibody is the first-line serological screening test for coeliac disease, with sensitivity ~95% and specificity ~98% in patients on a gluten-containing diet. It is cost-effective, widely available, and the test recommended by NICE and ESPGHAN guidelines as the single best initial serological investigation.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. IgA anti-tTG is the guideline-recommended first-line serology for coeliac disease in adults on a gluten-containing diet; it has the best combination of sensitivity, specificity, and cost-effectiveness.
EMA is highly specific (>99%) but less sensitive than anti-tTG, requires immunofluorescence (operator-dependent), and is used as a confirmatory second-line test rather than the initial screen.

Anti-gliadin antibodies have lower sensitivity and specificity than anti-tTG and have largely been abandoned from adult coeliac diagnostic protocols; they are mainly used in young children under 2 years.
Total serum IgA should be checked alongside anti-tTG to exclude IgA deficiency (which would give false-negative IgA-based tests), but it is not itself a diagnostic test for coeliac disease.
HLA-DQ2/DQ8 genotyping has high negative predictive value and can exclude coeliac disease, but it is positive in ~30% of the general population — it is used to rule out, not confirm, the diagnosis.
clinicalPearl: Always check total serum IgA alongside anti-tTG to exclude selective IgA deficiency, which affects ~2-3% of coeliac patients and would cause a false-negative IgA anti-tTG result.
keyClue: Iron-deficiency and folate-deficiency anaemia combined with loose offensive stools and bloating in a young woman on a gluten-containing diet.
commonExamTrap: Choosing HLA-DQ2/DQ8 as the diagnostic test — it only excludes, not confirms, coeliac disease.
missedConcept: The requirement to be on a gluten-containing diet before serological testing — a gluten-free diet normalises antibodies and causes false negatives.

clinicalStem: A 54-year-old man with a 20-year history of heavy alcohol use presents with recurrent epigastric pain radiating to the back, 10 kg weight loss over 6 months, and pale, bulky, foul-smelling stools that are difficult to flush. Blood tests: Hb 11.5 g/dL, serum amylase normal, albumin 28 g/L, random blood glucose 14.2 mmol/L. Plain abdominal X-ray shows calcification in the epigastric region. What is the SINGLE MOST LIKELY diagnosis?
explanation: Chronic pancreatitis classically presents with recurrent epigastric pain radiating to the back (often alcohol-related), exocrine insufficiency causing steatorrhoea (pale, bulky, offensive stools), and endocrine insufficiency causing diabetes (elevated glucose). Pancreatic calcification on plain X-ray is pathognomonic of chronic pancreatitis and distinguishes this from pancreatic cancer.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Pancreatic cancer should be considered but calcification on plain X-ray is strongly associated with chronic pancreatitis, not cancer. Cancer does not typically produce calcifications visible on plain X-ray.

Correct. The combination of alcohol history, pancreatic calcification (pathognomonic), steatorrhoea, diabetes, and recurrent epigastric pain is the hallmark presentation of chronic pancreatitis with exocrine and endocrine insufficiency.
Coeliac disease causes steatorrhoea and weight loss but does not explain epigastric pain radiating to the back, pancreatic calcification, or new-onset hyperglycaemia.
Primary biliary cholangitis causes fat-soluble vitamin deficiencies and cholestatic liver disease; it does not produce pancreatic calcification or explain this clinical triad.

Bacterial overgrowth can cause steatorrhoea and weight loss but would not produce pancreatic calcification or hyperglycaemia, and is typically seen in anatomical gut abnormalities or immunodeficiency.
clinicalPearl: Pancreatic calcification on plain abdominal X-ray is pathognomonic of chronic pancreatitis; the classic triad is steatorrhoea, diabetes mellitus, and recurrent epigastric pain in an alcohol-dependent patient.
keyClue: Pancreatic calcification on plain X-ray combined with steatorrhoea and new-onset hyperglycaemia in a heavy alcohol user.
commonExamTrap: Attributing steatorrhoea only to coeliac disease while overlooking the pancreatic calcification and hyperglycaemia pointing to chronic pancreatitis.
missedConcept: The dual (exocrine + endocrine) insufficiency of chronic pancreatitis and the significance of pancreatic calcification as a diagnostic X-ray finding.

clinicalStem: A 35-year-old Indian woman who returned from a 6-month voluntary placement in rural South Asia 3 months ago presents with persistent watery diarrhoea, abdominal cramps, weight loss, and glossitis. Blood tests: Hb 9.6 g/dL (macrocytic), folate 1.4 nmol/L, B12 112 pmol/L, IgA anti-tTG antibody negative. Stool microscopy reveals no parasites. Duodenal biopsy shows subtotal villous atrophy with crypt hyperplasia. What is the SINGLE MOST LIKELY diagnosis?
explanation: Tropical sprue occurs in individuals living in or returning from endemic tropical regions (South and Southeast Asia, Caribbean). It presents with chronic diarrhoea, macrocytic anaemia from folate and B12 deficiency, glossitis, and villous atrophy on biopsy. Crucially, anti-tTG antibodies are negative (differentiating it from coeliac disease). The combination of tropical travel, negative coeliac serology, and multi-nutrient deficiency confirms tropical sprue.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Coeliac disease causes identical histology (villous atrophy) but requires positive anti-tTG/EMA serology or response to a gluten-free diet for diagnosis; anti-tTG is negative here, effectively excluding coeliac disease.

Whipple's disease (Tropheryma whipplei) also causes malabsorption and villous atrophy but is associated with arthralgia, CNS symptoms, and PAS-positive macrophages on biopsy — none of which are present here.

Correct. Tropical sprue is the diagnosis: travel to an endemic region, chronic diarrhoea, macrocytic anaemia (folate + B12 deficiency), glossitis, villous atrophy on biopsy, and negative coeliac serology.

Giardia lamblia causes diarrhoea and malabsorption but typically does not cause significant villous atrophy or macrocytic anaemia; stool microscopy would show cysts/trophozoites, which are absent here.

Crohn's disease can cause small bowel inflammation and malabsorption but would not be expected to cause diffuse subtotal villous atrophy and prominent B12/folate deficiency in a returning traveller with no prior GI history.
clinicalPearl: Negative anti-tTG in the presence of villous atrophy should prompt consideration of tropical sprue in a returning traveller — treatment is tetracycline plus folate supplementation.
keyClue: Negative anti-tTG antibody + villous atrophy + macrocytic anaemia (folate + B12 deficiency) + recent prolonged stay in tropical South Asia.
commonExamTrap: Diagnosing coeliac disease based on villous atrophy alone without noting the negative serology and tropical travel history.
missedConcept: Tropical sprue as a cause of villous atrophy that mimics coeliac histology but is diagnosed by travel history and negative coeliac serology.

clinicalStem: A 22-year-old male student returns from a 4-week backpacking trip to Southeast Asia. He presents with a 10-day history of malaise, anorexia, nausea, and yellow discolouration of the eyes. He denies IV drug use or risky sexual behaviour. Examination shows jaundice, tender hepatomegaly, and no splenomegaly. Blood tests: ALT 1840 IU/L, AST 1620 IU/L, ALP 180 IU/L, bilirubin 68 µmol/L, INR 1.1. Which investigation result would MOST SPECIFICALLY confirm the diagnosis?
options: Positive hepatitis C RNA by PCR
explanation: Hepatitis A is the most likely diagnosis given the travel history to an endemic region, faeco-oral transmission route, acute hepatitic picture, young age, and no risk factors for blood-borne viruses. IgM anti-HAV appears at the onset of symptoms and remains positive for 3-6 months — it is the serological marker of ACUTE hepatitis A infection. IgG anti-HAV indicates past infection or vaccination.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. IgM anti-HAV is the definitive marker of acute hepatitis A infection; it is present from symptom onset and confirms current active infection.
HBsAg would indicate acute or chronic hepatitis B infection; the absence of IV drug use, risky sex, or prior blood exposure makes hepatitis B less likely, and HBsAg does not confirm hepatitis A.
Elevated ferritin >1000 suggests haemochromatosis or acute liver necrosis but is non-specific; it does not confirm hepatitis A.
Hepatitis C RNA by PCR detects hepatitis C viraemia; the incubation period is typically 6-12 weeks and sexual transmission is uncommon — hepatitis C is not the most likely diagnosis here.
Hepatitis A IgG indicates past infection or successful vaccination, not acute infection; it would be present in someone who had previously had hepatitis A and would not confirm the current illness.
clinicalPearl: In acute hepatitis with faeco-oral risk factors and travel to endemic regions, IgM anti-HAV confirms the diagnosis; IgG anti-HAV only confirms past exposure or immunity.
keyClue: Young traveller returning from Southeast Asia with acute hepatitic picture, no blood-borne virus risk factors — hepatitis A is the most likely diagnosis.
commonExamTrap: Selecting IgG anti-HAV — this indicates past exposure or immunity, not current acute infection.
missedConcept: The distinction between IgM (acute infection) and IgG (past infection/immunity) in viral hepatitis serology.

clinicalStem: A 32-year-old woman is brought to A&E after ingesting approximately 40 paracetamol tablets (20 g) 36 hours ago. She did not seek medical attention at the time. She is now confused and jaundiced. Investigations: bilirubin 210 µmol/L, INR 6.8, creatinine 340 µmol/L, pH 7.22 (arterial), ALT 4200 IU/L, blood glucose 2.8 mmol/L. What is the SINGLE MOST IMPORTANT next step in management?
options: Refer urgently to a liver transplant centre
Commence haemodialysis for acute kidney injury
explanation: This patient meets the King's College Criteria for poor prognosis in paracetamol-induced acute liver failure: arterial pH <7.30 alone is sufficient, or the combination of INR >6.5, creatinine >300 µmol/L, and grade III-IV encephalopathy (confusion). She meets both criteria. Urgent referral to a specialist liver transplant centre is the single most critical step — transplantation may be life-saving. NAC should also be continued but referral is the priority action.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Oral methionine is an alternative to NAC only when NAC is unavailable and must be given within 10 hours of overdose; it has no role at 36 hours post-ingestion with established acute liver failure.

Correct. With an arterial pH <7.30 (7.22) and the triad of INR >6.5, creatinine >300 µmol/L, and encephalopathy, this patient meets King's College Criteria and urgent transplant centre referral is the priority action.

IV N-acetylcysteine should be given (it may still have hepatoprotective benefit in established ALF) but the patient has already developed fulminant hepatic failure with acidosis — NAC alone is insufficient and transplant referral cannot be delayed.
Haemodialysis may be required for the AKI but it is supportive care; it does not address the underlying hepatic failure and is not the most important single step.
Upper GI endoscopy has no indication in this clinical scenario of paracetamol-induced acute liver failure without evidence of GI bleeding.
clinicalPearl: Arterial pH <7.30 after resuscitation in paracetamol-induced acute liver failure alone meets King's College Criteria for liver transplant referral — this supersedes all other management priorities.
keyClue: Arterial pH 7.22 + INR 6.8 + creatinine 340 + encephalopathy = King's College Criteria met — urgent transplant referral.
commonExamTrap: Prioritising N-acetylcysteine or haemodialysis over transplant referral — NAC is too late to reverse established liver failure meeting transplant criteria.
missedConcept: King's College Criteria for poor prognosis in paracetamol-induced ALF and the critical importance of urgent transplant centre referral.
redFlagReason: Acute liver failure with arterial pH <7.30, coagulopathy, renal failure, and encephalopathy is immediately life-threatening; delayed transplant referral is associated with preventable death.

clinicalStem: A 40-year-old man who emigrated from sub-Saharan Africa is found to have mildly elevated ALT (68 IU/L) on routine blood tests. He is asymptomatic. Hepatitis serology: HBsAg positive, anti-HBs negative, HBeAg negative, anti-HBe positive, anti-HBc IgG positive, anti-HBc IgM negative, HBV DNA 350 IU/mL. What does this serological pattern MOST LIKELY represent?
options: Resolved hepatitis B infection with natural immunity
Inactive chronic hepatitis B carrier state
explanation: The serological pattern — HBsAg positive, anti-HBs negative, HBeAg negative, anti-HBe positive, anti-HBc IgG positive (not IgM), low HBV DNA — represents the inactive chronic hepatitis B carrier state (previously called e-antigen negative chronic HBV). HBsAg persists >6 months, IgM anti-HBc is negative (excluding acute infection), and low DNA with seroconversion to anti-HBe indicates relatively quiescent infection.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Acute hepatitis B would show positive anti-HBc IgM and typically HBeAg positivity with high HBV DNA; IgM anti-HBc is negative here, excluding acute infection.
Resolved infection shows HBsAg negative, anti-HBs positive, anti-HBc IgG positive; here HBsAg is still present, confirming persistent infection rather than resolution.
Correct. HBsAg positive + anti-HBc IgG (not IgM) + anti-HBe positive + HBeAg negative + low HBV DNA = inactive chronic HBV carrier state (e-antigen negative chronic infection).
The window period is characterised by the disappearance of HBsAg and the appearance of anti-HBc IgM before anti-HBs develops; HBsAg positivity here excludes the window period.
Successful vaccination produces only anti-HBs positivity (isolated); it does not produce anti-HBc antibodies, and HBsAg would be absent.
clinicalPearl: The inactive HBV carrier state: HBsAg+, anti-HBs-, HBeAg-, anti-HBe+, anti-HBc IgG+, low or undetectable HBV DNA — these patients still need 6-monthly ALT and annual HBV DNA monitoring for reactivation.
keyClue: HBsAg positive + IgM anti-HBc NEGATIVE + anti-HBe positive + low HBV DNA = chronic (not acute) infection in the e-antigen negative phase.
commonExamTrap: Diagnosing resolved infection — resolved HBV has HBsAg negative; here HBsAg is positive, confirming persistent chronic infection.
missedConcept: The full interpretation of the HBV serology panel, particularly the critical role of anti-HBc IgM in distinguishing acute from chronic infection.

clinicalStem: A 48-year-old man with a long history of alcohol dependence presents with a 2-week history of progressive jaundice, tender hepatomegaly, and low-grade fever. He has continued drinking until 4 days ago. Bloods: bilirubin 220 µmol/L, AST 180 IU/L, ALT 70 IU/L, AST:ALT ratio 2.6:1, WCC 18.2 x10⁹/L, INR 2.1, albumin 24 g/L, creatinine 98 µmol/L. Ultrasound abdomen: enlarged echogenic liver, no biliary dilatation. What scoring system is MOST APPROPRIATE to determine whether this patient requires corticosteroid therapy?
explanation: Maddrey's Discriminant Function (DF) is the most widely validated scoring tool for determining corticosteroid eligibility in alcoholic hepatitis. DF = 4.6 × (PT prolongation in seconds) + bilirubin (µmol/L)/17.1. A score ≥32 indicates severe disease and a potential benefit from prednisolone therapy (NNT ~5 for 28-day mortality reduction). This patient's bilirubin and coagulopathy already suggest a high DF.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Child-Pugh score assesses the severity of chronic liver disease (cirrhosis) and prognosis, not the indication for corticosteroids in acute alcoholic hepatitis.
Correct. Maddrey's discriminant function (DF ≥32) is the primary tool used to stratify severe alcoholic hepatitis and determine eligibility for prednisolone therapy.

MELD score is widely used for liver transplant listing and prognosis in chronic liver disease; while it has been applied in alcoholic hepatitis research, Maddrey's DF remains the primary clinical tool for corticosteroid decision-making.

The Glasgow Alcoholic Hepatitis Score is a validated alternative to Maddrey's DF and some guidelines use it; however, Maddrey's DF is the more established and widely cited first-line tool for corticosteroid eligibility decisions.
APACHE II is an ICU severity scoring system used for critically ill patients broadly and has no specific role in guiding corticosteroid therapy for alcoholic hepatitis.
clinicalPearl: Maddrey's DF ≥32 in alcoholic hepatitis identifies severe disease and patients who may benefit from prednisolone; always exclude active infection before starting steroids.
keyClue: AST:ALT ratio >2:1 + jaundice + coagulopathy + alcohol history = alcoholic hepatitis; Maddrey's DF guides the corticosteroid decision.
commonExamTrap: Using Child-Pugh score for acute alcoholic hepatitis — Child-Pugh is for chronic liver disease severity, not acute alcoholic hepatitis steroid triage.
missedConcept: The role of Maddrey's discriminant function (DF ≥32) as the threshold for corticosteroid consideration in severe alcoholic hepatitis.

clinicalStem: A 55-year-old woman with a 15-year history of heavy alcohol consumption (>40 units/week) undergoes routine assessment. She is asymptomatic. Examination reveals a smooth, non-tender, mildly enlarged liver. Blood tests: ALT 58 IU/L, AST 72 IU/L, GGT 198 IU/L, bilirubin 18 µmol/L, INR 1.0, albumin 41 g/L. Ultrasound shows a hyperechoic liver. She denies any recent illness or jaundice. What is the MOST LIKELY diagnosis?
explanation: Alcoholic steatosis (fatty liver) is the earliest and most common stage of alcoholic liver disease, occurring in up to 90% of heavy drinkers. It is characterised by an asymptomatic enlarged, smooth liver, mildly elevated transaminases (AST:ALT <2:1 in steatosis), elevated GGT, and hyperechoic appearance on ultrasound. Liver synthetic function (INR, albumin) is preserved. It is fully reversible with alcohol abstinence.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Cirrhosis would present with signs of portal hypertension (splenomegaly, ascites, varices), reduced albumin, and deranged INR; synthetic function is preserved here and the liver is smooth rather than nodular.

Alcoholic hepatitis presents with acute jaundice, tender hepatomegaly, fever, and a markedly elevated bilirubin with leucocytosis; this patient is asymptomatic with near-normal bilirubin and no fever.

Correct. Asymptomatic hepatomegaly, mildly elevated transaminases, elevated GGT, preserved synthetic function, and hyperechoic liver on ultrasound in a heavy drinker = alcoholic steatosis, the earliest reversible stage of ALD.

NAFLD has an identical ultrasound appearance but occurs in the context of metabolic risk factors (obesity, diabetes, dyslipidaemia); this patient's heavy alcohol history (>40 units/week) makes alcoholic steatosis far more likely.

Hepatitis C would be expected to show more significant hepatocellular inflammation (higher ALT) and would not explain the hyperechoic liver on ultrasound; specific serology would be needed to diagnose HCV.
clinicalPearl: Alcoholic steatosis is the earliest, most common, and fully reversible stage of ALD — it presents with asymptomatic hepatomegaly, elevated GGT, mild transaminitis, and hyperechoic liver on ultrasound. Abstinence leads to complete resolution.
keyClue: Asymptomatic + smooth hepatomegaly + elevated GGT + hyperechoic liver + preserved synthetic function in a long-term heavy drinker = steatosis.
commonExamTrap: Over-diagnosing cirrhosis based solely on alcohol history — cirrhosis requires evidence of synthetic dysfunction or portal hypertension.
missedConcept: The three stages of alcoholic liver disease (steatosis → alcoholic hepatitis → cirrhosis) and their distinguishing clinical and biochemical features.

clinicalStem: A 58-year-old man with known alcoholic cirrhosis and ascites is admitted with a 2-day history of worsening abdominal pain, fever (38.6°C), and confusion. On examination, he is jaundiced with shifting dullness and diffuse abdominal tenderness. Bloods: WCC 16.4 x10⁹/L, CRP 94 mg/L, creatinine 168 µmol/L, albumin 22 g/L. Diagnostic paracentesis is performed. Which ascitic fluid result would be MOST DIAGNOSTIC of the likely complication?
options: Ascitic protein >25 g/L with SAAG <11 g/L
Ascitic fluid LDH greater than serum LDH
Turbid ascitic fluid with gram-negative rods on direct microscopy
explanation: Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP) is the most likely complication in a cirrhotic patient with ascites presenting with fever, abdominal pain, and encephalopathy. The diagnostic criterion for SBP is an ascitic fluid PMN (neutrophil) count >250 cells/mm³, regardless of culture result. Empirical antibiotics (typically third-generation cephalosporins) should be started immediately without waiting for culture results.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Ascitic protein >25 g/L with SAAG <11 g/L suggests exudative ascites from non-hepatic causes (malignancy, TB peritonitis); SBP occurs in cirrhotic (transudate) ascites with SAAG >11 g/L.
Correct. Ascitic PMN count >250 cells/mm³ is the diagnostic criterion for SBP — antibiotics must be started immediately on this result, without waiting for culture confirmation.
Ascitic amylase >1000 IU/L indicates pancreatic ascites from acute or chronic pancreatitis with pancreatic duct disruption; this is not the expected finding in SBP.
Ascitic LDH greater than serum LDH, with high protein and low glucose, characterises secondary peritonitis from gut perforation; it is used to distinguish secondary peritonitis from SBP.
Positive cultures on microscopy would support infection but are positive in only 40-60% of SBP cases; diagnosis rests on PMN count >250 cells/mm³, not culture positivity.
clinicalPearl: In cirrhotic ascites, PMN count >250 cells/mm³ on diagnostic tap = SBP; start cefotaxime immediately — do not wait for culture results. Give albumin 1.5 g/kg on day 1 to prevent hepatorenal syndrome.
keyClue: Cirrhotic patient with ascites + fever + abdominal pain + confusion = SBP until proven otherwise; PMN >250/mm³ is diagnostic.
commonExamTrap: Waiting for positive cultures before diagnosing SBP — cultures are negative in ~40-60% of cases; PMN count is the diagnostic criterion.
missedConcept: The PMN count >250 cells/mm³ diagnostic threshold for SBP and the need for empirical antibiotics plus IV albumin without waiting for culture.
redFlagReason: SBP carries 30-day mortality of 20-40% in cirrhotic patients; delayed antibiotic therapy and failure to give IV albumin significantly worsen outcomes, including hepatorenal syndrome.
